shortDescription.text: User-controlled Jinja2 template string passed to Template() constructor

fullDescription.text: The 'template' query parameter is passed directly to jinja2.Template(), allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary Jinja2 expressions. This enables full server-side template injection, which in Jinja2 can escalate to arbitrary code execution via __class__.__mro__ traversal (e.g., {{ ''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__() }}).

shortDescription.text: Result of user-controlled template rendered with |safe filter, disabling autoescaping

fullDescription.text: The rendered output of a user-controlled Jinja2 template is passed through the |safe filter (or Markup), which disables HTML autoescaping. Even if the SSTI itself were mitigated, rendering untrusted output as safe HTML enables cross-site scripting.
jinja2.Template() is used instead of jinja2.sandbox.SandboxedEnvironment. Without sandboxing, template expressions can access arbitrary Python objects and execute system commands.

message.text: User-supplied query parameter 'template' is passed directly to jinja2.Template() at line 450, enabling server-side template injection. An attacker can craft a payload such as {{ config.items() }} or {{ ''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__() }} to achieve remote code execution.

The output of t.render(name=name), where 't' is a user-controlled Jinja2 Template, is rendered with the |safe filter in the response template. This disables autoescaping and allows any HTML/JS payload produced by the SSTI to execute in the victim's browser, compounding SSTI with reflected XSS.

jinja2.Template() is instantiated without a SandboxedEnvironment. Even if input validation were added, the lack of sandboxing means any bypass would immediately grant full code execution. Defence-in-depth requires using jinja2.sandbox.SandboxedEnvironment.
